sentences.text: My name is Ludwig Weiler, spelled W-E-I-L-E-R, the surname.
Ludwig is my first name.
Originally, where I was born being in Hungary, coming from the place called Sopron-- in German, Odenburg-- on the Austro-Hungarian border.
I was called in Hungarian 'Lajas,' which is the equivalent of Ludwig.
And my nickname, if you like to call it that, was Laci.
Lajas is spelled L-A-J-A-S.
And Laci is L-A-C-I.
I was born on the 14th of March, 1930, being the Hebrew date of Purim.
And consequently, I'm just over 66 by a couple of months.
It was Sopron, Odenburg.
And it was Hungary, on the border town of Austria.
Yes.
Even though I was 14 years of age when we were deported in July 1944, I do have quite vivid recollection from approximately 1936.
And I do remember, very clearly, listening to a radio, which we had at the time of the Duke of Windsor, as he was known later, Edward the XIII's abdication.
And that is basically a very clear event, historical event, which I do remember.
Subsequently to that, maybe due to the unusual circumstances of the '30s, and obviously in the '40s, one was what one might call today pre-aged.
And one got into conversation with adults on daily events, which-- maybe not everybody.
But in my case, I think it has had an influence of being aware of what we knew, which of course wasn't everything which was happening.
I could basically start from the time I started school in Sopron.
There was an Orthodox and Neolog community, as it was from the time in the 1870s.
They split the communities, except for about two, three towns where they had what they called Status Quo communities, which meant they did not split.
And being a city of about 60,000 people, there were approximately 1,800 Jewish souls.
That means from babies to old people.
So therefore, there was only one primary school, Jewish primary school, which both communities' children attended to, as it was legal and obligatory for people to go to school.

And interestingly enough, the arrangement in the school was, whilst it was mainly supervised or run by the Neolog community, nevertheless, the Orthodox community was accommodated by the mere fact that the girls sat in the front rows and the boys in behind.
It was a perfectly normal primary school.
It has not affected anybody's additional requirements to study any Hebrew subject.
Some were obviously taught.
Presumably, the Orthodox community required a little bit more, which then people, like in my case, we individually had private lessons.
Or some attended to a Talmud Torah, which was also in the school in town.
The actual school was situated pretty well in the center of town, next to the Neolog shul, which was quite a nice edifice.
It doesn't exist today because it was bombed.
And presently, I think there is a block of flats being built on it.
We lived in a very nice part of town, which was called [INAUDIBLE].
It was a park, which had houses and residences on either side of the park.
The family originate not very far from Sopron, a place called Ebergoetz, which, in my childhood, I visited only once or twice.
The history-- this is basically a family story-- is that, as you know, in the Austro-Hungarian Empire, Jews were only allowed to live in the small villages up to the time of emancipation.
And at that time, my grandparents-- that means my paternal, my father's grandparents-- decided that they did not want to bring up the children in a village-like atmosphere.
And my grandfather had a brother who stayed in the place where they were the only merchants.
They owned the pub and had agricultural holdings and so on.
But as far as I was told, my grandfather sold it all out, moved to Sopron in the 1880s, and went into a jewelry business, which he had a successful business there.
And the family consisted-- my-- including my father, they were four brothers and two sisters, my father being the third oldest.
The attitude of the family was that you have to be religious, but at the same time be educated enough.
As in the world generally, it's difficult enough to exist.
So they were quite highly educated.
In fact, my father also finished a higher schooling and university, and later practiced in banking.
And he was a chartered accountant.
Even so, he was a businessman.
His older brother was a lawyer, Dr.
Bela Weiler, who was, for many, many years, the Rosh Hakol.
That means the head of the Orthodox community-- a modern man.
And he, in fact, was one of the foremost lawyers in western Hungary.
His other brother was a doctor who went to university in Vienna and practiced in Baden bei Wien, which is very close by.
And the fourth brother, who was actually the second oldest, went into the grandfather's business.
The two sisters-- the one sister, her name was Sidonia.

She was already an elderly lady in my childhood-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was married to a gentleman called Heitler, Gyula-- or Julius in German-- Heitler, who had a successful shoe shop in the main thoroughfare of the town.
Having had one son who was also considerably older than my generation, and was also deported with his wife and their young child.
The youngest being my aunt called Margit Weiler, she was married to an industrialist in a town called Papa, which was also in western Hungary, not very far from our town.
Today, it's basically an hour's drive.
And she is alive.
She has a son of my age and a daughter who is two years younger than me.

They were-- basically survived because, as you know, in the time of 1944, after the Germans came in on the 19th of March, the important people in every town, about 10 of them generally, depending on the size of the town, were immediately arrested.
This was a kind of a frightening move.
And also to chop off the head of the communal leaders and, therefore, disrupt the orderly management of the community, and advice of these same people.
My uncle, the lawyer, was one who was arrested immediately, practically a day or two after Germans marched in.
And so was my aunt's husband, being an important industrialist in their town of Papa.
And these people were kept in the local Gestapo headquarters.
And I have personal knowledge of the circumstances how they were kept, so much so that they did allow kosher food to be delivered to the inmates.
And I-- Even though I was a nephew and my uncle had a son of two years older than me, but probably, it was considered, at the age of 14, I was safer.
And even if I say so, I wasn't a terribly worried person those days even.
And I regularly went to the Gestapo every day and delivered, at lunchtime, the food and left there some food for the evening.
And whilst I was waiting to get the dishes back, I had the opportunity to watch the activities there.
One of the matters which even today is clearly in my mind-- that literally 5 or 7 minutes' walk from our house was a villa which the Gestapo requisitioned, a Jewish villa.

And it was absolutely amazing to see how they set up what they called the Thousand-Year Reich in the spring of 1944, when the whole world knew that the war was not going to last forever, and they were definitely on the losing end of it.

But they literally refurnished, brand newly carpeted and, in every conceivable comfort, prepared this villa for the Gestapo, which of course, all the goods and services were delivered by the local Jewish community-- needless to say, not against payment.

And whenever, these days, I see people overspending on their own houses, I always say that reminds me that they are behaving like the Gestapo, [LAUGHS] that they, regardless of the cost, carry on, which is not really my idea of living.
This is the period immediately after the 19th of March, 1944.

Now the reason I somewhat shifted into this field was because I was trying to explain that members of the family in our town-- the-- so if I go back, historically speaking, the family, according to the information I had at home-- was that while the family originated from Bohemia, as you probably know-- it's written in many history books, for those who care to look for it, that in Bohemia and Moravia, which is today the Czech Republic, was one of the most learned and well-to-do Jewish communities in Europe in the times of Maria Theresa, which was in the 1700s.

Except that the community got decimated by the anti-Jewish edicts given out by Maria Theresa, who was a singularly capable Jew hater, to the extent that there is an anecdote that she swore to herself never to set eye on a Jew.
And when, in one of her wars, she needed some extraordinary sum of money, she nevertheless called [INAUDIBLE] from Frankfurt.
And she erected a sheet of linen.
Like in the Orthodox weddings these days, they do a mechitza.
And while she took his money-- or the government, it was accepted to take loans, she refused to ever look at him.
Nevertheless, this was presumably acceptable to Jews those days.

And the-- the point I was trying to make is that the community got decimated by one of the most serious anti-Jewish regulations, which were voting to force that in every Jewish family, only one child who got married got permission of residence, whether it was in Prague, Brunn, or whatever place in the Czech Republic, Bohemia and Moravia.
And anybody else, any other children could stay there, provided they were not married.
In other words, to limit the Jewish population or basically eradicate it over the generations without killing them.
So there was immediately a consistent emigration from these provinces into other parts of the Austro-Hungarian Empire.
And the story goes that our family moved in the early 1800s from there into western Hungary, come Burgenland, which is the area where we lived.
And in fact, there is a famous seven communities, Sheva Kehillot, which many people know about in the eastern part of Austria-- Burgenland.
And those communities were basically populated and established by the Esterhazy family, who were very liberal to Jews and held very large estates on both sides of the border in Austria and Hungary.
I know that one of the rogues of the family was the Esterhazy involved in the Dreyfus Affair.
It was actually, as far as the family was concerned, a throw-out.
So the family, as such, were decent to Jews.
And they allowed them to settle in these seven communities.
There were certain restrictions.
It wasn't all that free for all.
There were many raids by the Catholic Church, where they were trying to grab children and whatnot.
There was a very personalized history of my Weiler grandmother, whose maiden name was Heitler, that there was a child, apparently, who lost his parents at a very early age.
And the local Catholic priest tried to pressure, as a community, that as the child has got no guardian, they want to take the child into care.
And they put him with a non-Jewish family.
And then, at the end, protestations went to Vienna to the, at the time, kaiser, or whoever was in charge.
And he decided, in a Solomon-type of decision, that they will have to create in the certain part of that town two walls, human walls, one where the Jews are and one where the non-Jews are.
And whichever side the child is going to go, that's where it belongs.
Anyway, the story goes on that the child came-- I don't know, a three- or four-year-old child-- and recognized an aunt or whoever and went there.
And that was so.
What I'm trying to bring out is that whilst the Esterhazys, as such, were liberal to Jews, the entire atmosphere was far from liberal.
But I presume, in other parts of Europe, probably it wasn't much better either.
Now the-- my maternal side of the family-- the family name is Holzer, H-O-L-Z-E-R-- also similarly came to that part of Hungary in around about the 1700s, 1800s.
And according to the anecdotes of the family, they originated from the Rhineland.
Bearing the name of Holzer would probably justify it.

And they lived in a certain part of the next county, which is called Vas Megye, spelled V-A-S, and moved into the capital of that county called Szombathely, which is 60 kilometers south of Sopron, the town I was born in.
And having come, again, from agriculture background and having liquidated their assets there-- went into the business of, of a flour mill, which they were very, very successful.
In fact, my grandfather, for many-- most of the years I remember, was a president of the community and a very charitable man and known to be a, a very wealthy man.
So I'm giving you basically the background of the family.
My mother had two sisters and four brothers.
Two sisters, unfortunately, died before the war, one in 1930-- I didn't even know her-- apparently from some food poisoning.
And the other sister in-- her name was Sarah Holzer.
She died in 1935, apparently of some cancer.
I remember her very clearly because my uncle, the doctor, the Weiler uncle, was very active in taking her to specialists in Vienna, which was known to be an important medical center.
Even so, we had hospitals and doctors.
But obviously, like today, in bigger cities, the care is better.
And I was, in 1935, '36, I remember standing at the window when they brought her with an ambulance back.
Probably by that time, they knew there was nothing to be done.
And these are sort of small matters which clearly stick in one's mind.
Now the history of our family is basically as I described it.
And then is more the contemporary part of it, which I can remember.
My father's occupation, having had all the educational background, was a merchant.
Before he got married-- he got married at a late station, when he was 31 years old.
But it was customary in our part of the world that you didn't get married unless you had a living to support your wife and family.
I suppose in some circles today, it's different.
However, let it be as it may.
The-- My father's first business and main business was being a wine exporter, because that area was very much a wine-growing district.
And very successfully, he conducted that business right up till the end of the First World War, when suddenly, overnight, the Austro-Hungarian monarchy broke up.
And duties were imposed between the previous empire, like Hungary and Austria.
And in so many words, the business became a nonstarter because of the duties imposed.
Because obviously, Austria produced wines as well, so-- the Pinot.
It's like the common market today versus any other economic blocks.
So my father, with the help of his older brother, the lawyer, Bela-- Dr., Dr.
Bela Weiler, had excellent connections in town.
And due to information which they received from the town hall and so on, they realized in which direction the town is going to develop.
It was always an important trading post, being a border town.
And it is a very old city of the Roman times.
And it was-- maybe historically, I should have put that first-- a Roman city called Scarbantia.
And I've seen once on a map that first ever Jewish settlements in Europe or even the European part of Russia were in that city.
And Buda-- because Pest was a much later developed twin city of Budapest, now it's called.
Because the western Hungary was called Pannonia in the Roman times.
And according to stories, the first Jews came with the Roman armies either as slaves or traders.
And I've seen a map-- in fact, it was just by coincidence-- where there were two blue dots showing.
And these were the two cities where the first Jews were known of.

Needless to say that the Hungarians who occupied the country 1,000 years ago were-- and came somewhere in the border of China and Manchuria or Russia, being a nomadic people, were somehow never very Jew-friendly.
But there were Jews living under maybe difficult conditions.
And in our city, I have a book, which was written even by a non-Jew, where they tell the story of the Jews being allowed into the city and trade, first temporarily, then permanently.
And each time, after a period of time when they became more well-to-do, they suddenly decided to chase them out from there and, therefore, confiscate their assets.
And this went on in various periods.

And in the Hungarian history, it is a black date in the Hungarian historical calendar of 1526, when the Turks beat the Hungarians in Mohacs, which is in southern Hungary and consequently occupied the country for 150 years.
The Turks, as you know, went up, right up to Vienna.
But in the Jewish records of history, it is known to be the only golden period the Jews ever experienced in the country of Hungary.
Well, you see, you must put things in the proper context of the time.
I grew up in what was generally a a sort of antisemitic but, nevertheless, in a indirect manner.
That means that people were allowed to work.
People were allowed to trade.
They were not restricted as such in their activities.
I'm talking, I presume, up to the mid '30s, and not later.
However, if I go back just 10 years before I was born, as you know, after the First World War Hungary had one of the first communist governments.
And that happened to have been led by a Jew by the name of Bela Kun.
And in 1920, Horthy, who was the head of state from that time right up till 1944, '45, was a-- what they called the White Terror.

And there were terrible atrocities against Jews committed in the name of the Hungarian people, simply by blaming the Jews for the short period of communism, which was quite like the Bolshevik Revolution.
Nevertheless, eventually that settled down and calmed down.

But there was an implied and actively pursued antisemitism by the virtue of the fact that from the 1920s onwards, there was numerous classes where Jews were not allowed to attend universities anymore, or in very small numbers.
In other words, they were immediately excluded of higher education and professional training.
In my time, they further extended this to apply to grammar schools.
In other words, after primary school, from the age of 10, you were excluded to attend the good schools.
And therefore, what I'm trying to indicate is that it affected one in the sense of one's life generally.
It was maybe not financially immediately.
But the direction was going into the way of a restrictive existence.
